title: Agentic Sales Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform that runs SDR agents — prospect research and enrichment, personalized outreach, CRM updates, and qualified handoff — where the action behind the gate is sending in the company's name. The case is scoped to email-first outbound (the channel with the richest deliverability and reputation mechanics); SMS/phone is a deferred extension that reuses the same gate behind channel-specific consent and throttling (see the channel-routing option in the send-gate step). On the irreversibility gradient it sits beside Marketing: the damage is sender reputation and deliverability (and a compliance violation), not money or filings, but a burned domain is slow to recover. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds two vertical-specific things: a CRM-plus-waterfall-enrichment grounding corpus, and a send gate (identity-resolved opt-out suppression, compliance, approval, and reputation-aware rate limiting) with deliverability as first-class infrastructure.

nodes.label: Sales Rep / Manager
nodes.description: Owns the pipeline; approves outreach and takes qualified handoffs.

nodes.label: Signal / Trigger
nodes.description: Intent signal, web event, or ICP list that starts an outreach task.

nodes.label: Sales API
nodes.description: Authenticates triggers, enforces tenant isolation, routes to the orchestrator.

nodes.label: SDR Orchestrator
nodes.description: Supervisor: research -> personalize -> sequence -> handoff. Hierarchical multi-agent.

nodes.label: Research / Outreach Agent
nodes.description: LLM node that enriches a prospect and drafts personalized outreach.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: CRM
nodes.description: System of record: bidirectional read/write with field-level permissions and conflict resolution.

nodes.label: Waterfall Enrichment
nodes.description: Queries data providers in sequence (primary -> fallback) with per-field gap detection and cost control.

nodes.label: Suppression / Identity Resolution
nodes.description: Global, identity-resolved opt-out and do-not-contact list surviving across domains and inboxes.

nodes.label: Deliverability Manager
nodes.description: Domain/inbox rotation, warmup, per-inbox caps, bounce/spam monitoring, SPF/DKIM/DMARC.

nodes.label: Send Gate
nodes.description: Final pre-send gate: suppression check, compliance, approval, reputation-aware rate limit.

nodes.label: Draft Sequence Store
nodes.description: Pending sequences and immutable approved draft versions; nothing sends until cleared.

nodes.label: Send Queue / Scheduler
nodes.description: Durable queue of approved sends; the deliverability manager leases work to it only when a warmed inbox has cap. Holds send-attempt lifecycle and provider-retry backoff.

nodes.label: Sending Infrastructure
nodes.description: Sending domains and inboxes; the actual email/channel delivery surface.

nodes.label: Policy & Compliance
nodes.description: CAN-SPAM/GDPR/TCPA, tenant isolation, and screening of untrusted enriched/web text.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access to the CRM and sending infra; ties approval to a rep.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only audit_events stream (actor, action, object, decision, policy/source provenance, ts): enrichment sources, drafts, suppression checks, approvals, sends — retained for compliance.

functional: Source prospects from intent signals and ICP lists; enrich each via a waterfall of data providers.
Personalize email-first outreach grounded in enriched, deduped CRM data (SMS/phone deferred, behind the same gate with channel-specific consent).
Update the CRM bidirectionally as the system of record.
Send only after a gate: suppression, compliance, approval, and reputation-aware rate limiting.
Hand qualified replies to a human rep.
nonFunctional: Protect sender reputation: deliverability is first-class (domain/inbox rotation, warmup, per-inbox caps, bounce/spam monitoring).
Honor every opt-out via a global, identity-resolved suppression list surviving across domains and inboxes.
Compliance: CAN-SPAM (address, opt-out) and GDPR for email; TCPA (phone/SMS) applies when the deferred channels are enabled.
Tenant isolation; enriched and web text treated as untrusted.
Auditable: enrichment sources, drafts, suppression checks, approvals, and sends recorded.

capacity.label: Workload
capacity.note: Assume 2k tenants, ~3 active campaigns each, ~500 new prospects/campaign/day -> ~3M prospects/day to enrich and sequence.

capacity.label: Enrichment fan-out
capacity.note: ~60% cache hit on warm prospects -> ~1.2M cache misses/day; the waterfall hits ~2.5 providers/miss -> ~3M provider calls/day (~35 QPS avg, ~100-150 peak). Provider QPS/cost caps force async enrichment + a refresh queue for low-priority prospects.

capacity.label: Send volume vs inbox caps
capacity.note: A warmed inbox safely sends ~40-50 cold emails/day; ramp starts ~10-20/day. ~3M prospects/day enter as first-touch emails, and multi-step sequences (~2-3 follow-ups each) push total volume higher still. Even at ~3M sends/day, ÷ ~45 ≈ ~67k warmed inboxes across rotated domains — the inbox cap, not provider throughput, is the binding constraint, and why sends queue and pace rather than fire on approval.

capacity.label: Reputation thresholds
capacity.note: Cross ~2% bounce or ~0.1% complaint on a domain and auto-pause it; these hard thresholds (not API quotas) drive cap reductions and quarantine.

capacity.label: Suppression
capacity.note: Opt-out must hold across every domain/inbox, identity-resolved; checked at submit and re-checked at send, so it needs an in-memory/indexed lookup well under provider submit latency.

capacity.label: Audit & events
capacity.note: Each prospect emits enrichment, draft, suppression-check, approval, send, and webhook events (~5-7 each); append-only audit + event stream sized for ~10-20M/day with long retention for compliance.

api.description: Start an outreach task; the orchestrator enriches, personalizes, and queues a sequence for approval.

A rep approves a specific draft version. The approval is idempotent (same key + version is a no-op) and snapshots the exact text approved; it enqueues the send rather than delivering directly, so the gate can re-check suppression and reputation at send time.

Provider callback for delivery events (delivered, bounced, complained, unsubscribed). Deduplicated on provider event id; unsubscribes and hard bounces write the global suppression list immediately and out-of-order events are reconciled by event timestamp.

Provider callback for an inbound reply. Deduped on provider event id; the orchestrator classifies intent before any rep handoff — replies never route straight to a rep. Unsubscribe/angry intent writes suppression and stops the sequence; qualified/pricing intent updates the CRM and hands off to the owning rep. Every branch appends an audit event.

Operator emergency kill switch. Pauses sending for a tenant, domain, or the whole fleet; queued attempts transition to blocked and the durable queue holds (work is paused, not dropped). Resumed via the same endpoint with action=resume. Audited with the operator identity.
Fetch reputation/deliverability metrics driving send caps.

dataModel.name: prospects
dataModel.note: Enriched, deduped prospect tied to the CRM (per-tenant).

dataModel.name: enrichment_results
dataModel.note: Per-field, per-provider enrichment with provenance — supports waterfall, freshness, and cost control.

dataModel.name: sequences
dataModel.note: An outreach campaign instance for one prospect (its steps live in sequence_messages).

dataModel.name: drafts
dataModel.note: Immutable approved draft versions — the exact text a rep approved.

dataModel.name: approvals
dataModel.note: Append-only approval snapshot tied to a rep and a specific draft version.

dataModel.name: send_attempts
dataModel.note: The send-attempt lifecycle: idempotent, provider-tracked, deduped on callbacks.

dataModel.name: inboxes
dataModel.note: Sending inbox/domain with warmup, auth, and live health.

dataModel.name: deliverability_events
dataModel.note: Deduped provider callbacks: bounces, complaints, unsubscribes, replies.

dataModel.name: suppression
dataModel.note: Identity-resolved opt-out / do-not-contact list. Composite unique on (scope, tenant_id, identity_key); tenant_id is NULL for platform scope. Gate blocks if a platform-scope row OR a matching tenant-scope row exists.

dataModel.name: audit_events
dataModel.note: First-class append-only audit trail (never updated/deleted): who approved what, which source data was used, which checks ran, and why a send was allowed or blocked. Retained for compliance; partitioned by ts.

dataModel.name: crm_sync
dataModel.note: Bidirectional CRM sync state for conflict resolution.

technologyChoices.concern: API edge, tenant auth, and campaign admission
tradeoff: The edge should authenticate tenant traffic, apply coarse campaign admission limits, and reject malformed or abusive triggers before they enter the orchestrator.
Self-hosted gateways give exact control over tenant headers and custom quotas; managed gateways reduce TLS, WAF, identity, and regional failover work.
Do not put sender-reputation caps only at the edge: per-inbox leases still belong in the deliverability scheduler.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed API edge with identity integration and WAF removes most custom reverse-proxy code, but not campaign-aware admission and send-time reputation checks.

technologyChoices.concern: SDR orchestration and durable workflow state

aws.name: Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow

tradeoff: The orchestrator coordinates research, enrichment, drafting, approval, send attempts, and reply classification, so long-running state and retries must survive worker restarts.
A workflow engine makes the approval/send/reply lifecycle explicit; a simple queue plus database is lighter but pushes state-machine correctness into application code.
Airflow-style batch orchestration is useful for refresh/backfill jobs, not for low-latency send gating.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed workflow service removes much of the custom retry timer and lease bookkeeping, but it still needs idempotency keys and tenant-aware policy checks.

technologyChoices.concern: Waterfall enrichment cache and provider refresh pipeline
tradeoff: The enrichment layer needs per-field provenance, confidence, freshness, and cost controls, not just a blob cache.
Redis or managed cache fits hot lookups; a relational or document store keeps the durable provenance needed for audit and CRM conflict resolution.
Async refresh queues smooth provider QPS and cost spikes but introduce freshness policy decisions.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A cache with TTLs plus a durable enrichment-results table removes repeated provider calls for warm prospects, but it does not remove source provenance or refresh scheduling.

tradeoff: Native CRM APIs give field-level control, external versions, and conflict metadata, but you own rate limits and sync state.
Managed iPaaS connectors speed integration but often hide conflict semantics that matter for safe bidirectional writes.
CDC is useful for internal projection updates; it does not replace CRM ownership and permission checks.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed connector can remove custom polling and OAuth refresh loops, but not the conflict-resolution policy that protects curated CRM fields.

technologyChoices.concern: LLM serving, prompt-injection guardrails, and model routing
tradeoff: The research agent can read enriched/web text, but that text must stay untrusted data and cannot carry tool authority or bypass the deterministic send gate.
Self-hosted inference gives cost and data-control leverage but increases model-serving, scaling, and safety-rail work.
Managed model platforms reduce serving operations and add safety controls, but model routing, tenant policy, and prompt-injection isolation remain application responsibilities.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Provider guardrail features can remove some custom content-classifier work, but not the architectural split between untrusted enrichment text and privileged send authority.

technologyChoices.concern: Drafts, approvals, and send-attempt transaction store
tradeoff: Immutable draft versions, approvals, send attempts, idempotency keys, and provider message IDs need transactional semantics.
A relational database keeps the lifecycle queryable and auditable; a key-value store can scale idempotency and attempt lookups but makes cross-entity review workflows harder.
Global SQL helps multi-region tenants but costs more and makes data-residency policy explicit.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A database with unique constraints or conditional writes removes many hand-built idempotency locks around approve/send retries.

tradeoff: An in-memory or indexed hot path hits the sub-10ms send-time check, but it needs a durable system of record behind it.
Keep the authoritative suppression table scoped by platform or tenant; replicate a hot identity index for the gate.
The lookup must be re-run at send time because approval can race with a later opt-out, hard bounce, or complaint.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A cache-backed lookup with durable write-through removes per-provider suppression hacks and campaign-local do-not-contact lists.

technologyChoices.concern: Send queue, reputation scheduler, and retry pipeline
tradeoff: The queue must be durable and support per-inbox lease/pace decisions, not just FIFO delivery.
A log or stream is strong for replaying lifecycle events; a work queue is simpler for visibility timeouts, retries, and DLQs.
A workflow engine makes provider timeouts and idempotent retry states explicit at the cost of another runtime.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed queue with native DLQ, visibility timeout, and scheduled retry support removes much of the custom lease and backoff machinery.

technologyChoices.concern: Email sending infrastructure and deliverability telemetry
tradeoff: Self-hosted MTAs give full control of IP pools, warmup, and rotation but make you own feedback loops, abuse handling, and deliverability operations.
Managed senders bootstrap authentication and feedback loops but cap per-IP/domain control and add per-message cost.
Cold-outreach volume still needs many warmed domains and inboxes regardless of provider; the inbox cap, not provider throughput, is the binding constraint.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed sender with bounce/complaint callbacks removes custom feedback-loop plumbing, but not the reputation-aware scheduler or global suppression gate.

technologyChoices.concern: Provider webhooks, event dedupe, and reply classification
tradeoff: Delivery callbacks and replies arrive duplicated, out of order, and sometimes after campaign state changed.
A durable event-id dedupe store protects bounce/complaint/unsubscribe handling; an event bus keeps classification, suppression, CRM update, and audit consumers decoupled.
Reply classification belongs before rep handoff so unsubscribe, angry, and out-of-office replies do not become false qualified leads.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed event ingress plus queue removes much of the custom webhook retry plumbing, but not provider-event reconciliation and reply-intent policy.

tradeoff: An append-only stream into cheap object or columnar storage is durable and queryable for compliance replay.
A ledger database gives stronger tamper-evidence but is harder to query and operate at high event volume.
Operational tables are not enough; audit needs actor, policy version, decision, object, provenance, and timestamp in one immutable trail.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed streaming plus immutable storage removes hand-rolled audit file rotation and retention jobs, but not the decision schema itself.

gcp.name: Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus

tradeoff: Deliverability metrics need real-time alerting on bounce/spam thresholds and historical trend analysis by tenant, domain, inbox, campaign, and provider.
LLM trajectory and reply-quality eval are separate from infrastructure metrics; production systems usually need both.
Trace IDs should connect enrichment, draft, approval, send attempt, provider callback, reply classification, CRM update, and audit append.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed metrics and tracing stack removes time-series operations, but not domain-specific SLOs for reputation, suppression correctness, and handoff quality.

patterns.name: Orchestrator-workers / scatter-gather subagents
patterns.what: A supervisor runs research -> personalize -> sequence as a hierarchical multi-agent flow.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-step SDR work across many prospects.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A rep approves outreach before it sends in the company's name.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: Agents research and draft freely but a deterministic send gate fences delivery.
patterns.whenToUse: High-volume outreach with a reputation/compliance boundary.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground personalization in enriched, deduped CRM data, the system of record.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: The agent acts via scoped, short-lived OAuth tokens (CRM write, send) carrying an act/approved-by claim naming the rep — not the rep's own credentials. Approval binds a specific rep to a specific draft version, so every send is attributable.
patterns.whenToUse: Writing the CRM and sending on the company's behalf.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Enriched and scraped web text is untrusted data, never instructions: it can inform a draft but cannot carry tool calls or bypass the send gate. A quarantined/Dual-LLM split keeps content the agent reads out of the privileged path that approves and sends.

patterns.name: Queue-based load leveling; admission control
patterns.what: Level outreach against per-inbox reputation caps, not just API limits.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate reply quality and qualification, and watch deliverability metrics.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Blasts Outreach (the baseline)
description: Start with the demo: an agent drafts an email per prospect and sends them all from one domain as fast as it can.

It burns the asset it depends on. One domain blasting unverified, un-personalized mail spikes bounces and spam complaints, tanks sender reputation, and lands future mail in spam — and it ignores opt-outs and compliance. Deliverability is the scarce resource, and the naive version destroys it: a burned domain takes weeks to recover, if it recovers at all.
The rest of the interview adds enrichment grounding, a send gate, and deliverability infrastructure.

view.caption: An agent blasts mail from one domain — the reputation-destroying baseline.

steps.decisionPrompt: What scarce resource does naive mass-sending destroy, and how fast does it recover?
whyNow: The naive blast makes deliverability and suppression the central requirements.

recap.after: An agent can send outreach at volume.
recap.newRisk: Sender reputation collapses, opt-outs are ignored, and there's no approval or compliance.

traps.trap: Maximizing send volume from one domain.
traps.why: Bounces and complaints burn reputation; a burned domain is slow to recover and lands you in spam.
traps.instead: Ground, gate, and rate-limit sends against reputation, with rotation and warmup.

steps.title: 2. Ground in the CRM + Waterfall Enrichment
description: The grounding corpus is the prospect data: the CRM as system of record, enriched through a waterfall — query data providers in sequence (primary then fallbacks), stopping at the first hit per field, with gap detection and cost control, then identity-resolve and dedupe against the CRM.
Personalization is grounded in this resolved profile, not in the model's guesses; enriched and scraped text is treated as untrusted.

view.caption: Prospects are enriched through a provider waterfall and deduped against the CRM (the system of record); untrusted enriched text is screened by the guardrail.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why query providers in a waterfall instead of one source, and where does dedup happen?

options.title: Synchronous waterfall (enrich inline)
options.summary: The research agent blocks on the provider waterfall before drafting.
pros: Simplest control flow; freshest data at draft time.
Easy to reason about per-prospect cost.
cons: Provider latency and outages stall the whole pipeline.
Burns provider budget even on low-priority prospects.
At ~35 QPS avg (and bursty peaks) of provider calls it doesn't fit per-provider QPS caps.

view.caption: Inline enrichment: simple, but provider latency/QPS caps bound throughput.

options.title: Async enrichment queue (recommended)
options.summary: Enrichment is decoupled behind a queue; drafting waits on resolved profiles, low-priority refresh runs in the background.
pros: Absorbs provider latency/outages and smooths bursts under per-provider QPS caps.
Lets cost control and field-level fallbacks run without blocking drafting.
Cache + freshness rules cut provider spend (~60% hit assumed).
cons: More moving parts; data may be slightly stale at draft time.

view.caption: Queued enrichment with caching/freshness: fits provider caps and isolates untrusted text.

concepts.definition: Querying data providers in sequence (primary -> fallback) per field, with gap detection and cost control.
concepts.whyItMatters: No single provider has full coverage; a waterfall reaches much higher coverage at controlled cost.
concepts.example: Email from provider A; if missing, try B, then C — stop at the first hit per field.

whyNow: Grounded, deduped data is what makes personalization credible and suppression accurate.

recap.after: Prospects are enriched, identity-resolved, and deduped against the CRM.
recap.newRisk: Even perfect personalization must not send to someone who opted out or burn the domain.

traps.trap: Letting enrichment overwrite CRM fields blindly.
traps.why: The CRM is the system of record; a low-confidence provider value can clobber a rep's hand-curated field and create sync conflicts.
traps.instead: Treat enrichment as a provenance-bearing input — write only on confidence + freshness thresholds, and resolve conflicts against the CRM's external version.

traps.trap: Feeding scraped/enriched web text straight into the prompt as instructions.
traps.why: Attacker-reachable content can carry prompt-injection that tries to bypass the send gate or exfiltrate data.
traps.instead: Treat enriched text as untrusted data, not instructions — screen it at the guardrail and keep it out of tool/send authority (Dual-LLM / CaMeL).

steps.title: 3. The Gate: Suppression, Compliance & Approve-Before-Send
description: This is the crux. Before any message leaves, a deterministic send gate runs three checks: the global, identity-resolved suppression list (opt-outs and do-not-contact survive across every domain and inbox), compliance (CAN-SPAM address/opt-out, GDPR, TCPA), and rep approval of the sequence.

Only an approved, suppression-cleared, compliant message proceeds to delivery. The gate protects sender reputation and legal standing — the analogue of the finance and legal gates, on the reputation tier.

view.caption: Approval creates an immutable draft version; the deterministic send gate then re-checks global suppression and compliance immediately before delivery.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you guarantee an opt-out honored on one domain blocks sends from every other inbox?

options.title: Approval = send (check once)
options.summary: Approval runs suppression/compliance once, then delivers directly.
pros: Fewer states; approval is the single decision point.
Lowest latency from approve to inbox.
cons: Loses the race: a prospect can opt out between approval and the queued send.
No re-check if reputation drops while work is queued.
Approval and delivery are conflated, so audit can't distinguish them.

view.caption: Single check at approval — simple, but races with late opt-outs and reputation changes.

options.title: Approval snapshot + final pre-send recheck (recommended)
options.summary: Approval freezes an immutable draft version and enqueues; the gate re-checks suppression, compliance, and cap availability immediately before provider submission, with an idempotency key on the send attempt.
pros: A late opt-out blocks the queued send — suppression is authoritative at send time.
Reputation pauses apply to already-queued work.
Idempotent approve/send + immutable snapshot make audit and retries safe.
cons: More states (approved/queued/blocked/sent) and a send-attempt store to operate.

view.caption: Immutable approved version + final recheck at send time closes the opt-out/reputation race.

patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: A do-not-contact / opt-out list keyed to a resolved identity and a scope (tenant or platform), enforced across all sending domains and inboxes. The gate blocks a send if a platform-scope row exists for the identity, OR a tenant-scope row exists for that identity and the sending tenant.
concepts.whyItMatters: Opt-outs are legal obligations and reputation-critical; per-inbox lists leak. Scope makes ‘this tenant only’ vs ‘the whole platform’ enforceable and queryable.
concepts.example: An unsubscribe on domain A suppresses the same person on domains B and C; a platform-scope abuse block suppresses them for every tenant.

whyNow: The gate is what makes high-volume agent outreach safe to send.

messages.label: re-check opt-out / DNC at send time

deepDives.title: Send-attempt lifecycle: states, idempotency, and races
points: States: awaiting_approval -> approved -> queued -> (blocked | sent) -> delivered | bounced | complained | unsubscribed | failed; plus paused_for_reputation for the whole sequence.
Approve and send both carry idempotency keys; a retried approval or send is a no-op, not a duplicate message.
Approval snapshots an immutable draft version, so the audit records exactly what a human approved.
Suppression and cap availability are re-checked at send time, not just at approval — this is what makes 'approved but not yet sent' safe.
Provider callbacks (delivered/bounce/complaint/unsubscribe) are deduped on provider event id and reconciled by timestamp when out of order.
A provider timeout after it accepted the message is resolved by the idempotency key on retry, not by re-sending blindly.
Stopping a campaign or a reputation pause transitions queued attempts to blocked before they fire.

deepDives.title: Where compliance state lives (so the gate is defensible)
points: Lawful basis / consent provenance: prospects.consent_basis on the prospect, with the source recorded in audit_events.
Footer/physical-address template version: drafts.footer_template_version, snapshotted into the immutable approved version so you can prove which CAN-SPAM footer shipped.
Policy-versioned checks: every gate decision writes audit_events.policy_version, so you can replay which compliance ruleset allowed or blocked a send.

Deletion / DSR requests: a deletion erases enriched PII (enrichment_results, prospect fields) but preserves a minimal suppression row (identity_key + reason) so the person stays opted-out after their data is gone.
Suppression provenance: suppression rows carry reason, source_channel, source_event_id, and actor — enough to defend why someone is on the list.

recap.before: Messages could send to anyone unchecked.
recap.after: Every send is suppression-checked, compliant, rep-approved against an immutable version, and re-checked at send time.
recap.newRisk: Even compliant sends burn reputation if volume and warmup aren't managed.

traps.why: Opt-outs then leak across domains, breaking the law and trust.
traps.instead: Maintain one global, identity-resolved suppression list checked at the gate.

traps.trap: Treating approval as delivery (check suppression once).
traps.why: A prospect can opt out, or reputation can drop, between approval and the queued send.
traps.instead: Snapshot an immutable approved version, enqueue, and re-check suppression + caps at send time.

traps.trap: Retrying a send (or approval) without an idempotency key.
traps.why: A provider timeout after it accepted the message double-sends on retry, and duplicate webhooks double-count.
traps.instead: Idempotency keys on approve/send and event-id dedup on provider callbacks.

steps.title: 4. Deliverability as First-Class Infrastructure
description: Reputation is engineered, not assumed. The deliverability manager rotates sending domains and inboxes, warms them up, enforces per-inbox volume caps, monitors bounce and spam-complaint rates against hard thresholds, and maintains SPF/DKIM/DMARC.

These reputation-driven rate limits are distinct from provider API limits: a fleet can sit well under its API quota and still burn a domain. They bound how fast the fleet may send, and the queue levels outreach against them.

view.caption: Approved sends wait in a durable queue; the deliverability manager rotates/warms inboxes, enforces per-inbox caps, monitors bounce/spam, and only leases queued work when a warmed inbox has cap.

steps.decisionPrompt: What signals throttle sending, and why are they different from API rate limits?

options.title: Provider API-limit scheduler
options.summary: Pace sends to the provider's published API quota.
pros: Trivial to implement; never gets 429'd by the provider.
cons: API quota has nothing to do with inbox reputation — you can stay under quota and still burn the domain.
No per-inbox warmup ramp; cold inboxes get blasted.

view.caption: API-quota pacing: easy, but quota ≠ reputation, so it still burns domains.

options.title: Reputation-aware per-inbox scheduler (recommended)
options.summary: Caps derive from each inbox's warmup state and live bounce/spam rates; the deliverability manager leases queued work only when a warmed inbox has headroom, and auto-pauses domains that cross thresholds.
pros: Caps track the real constraint — reputation, not API quota.
Per-inbox warmup ramp and auto-pause/quarantine on bad signals.
Queued work waits safely when caps are exhausted.
cons: Needs a control loop ingesting bounce/complaint/reply signals.
Lower peak throughput per inbox (by design).

view.caption: Reputation-aware control loop: caps from warmup + live signals, lease-on-headroom, auto-pause.

concepts.definition: Send caps derived from inbox warmup state and live bounce/spam rates, not from provider API quotas.
concepts.whyItMatters: Exceeding them silently lands future mail in spam; it's the real constraint on volume.
concepts.example: A new inbox starts at a low daily cap and ramps as it warms with clean engagement.

whyNow: Deliverability infrastructure is what lets the platform send at volume without self-destructing.

messages.label: adjust cap / lease next when headroom

deepDives.title: Deliverability control loop and pause runbook
points: Inputs: bounce, complaint, and reply events per inbox/domain; outputs: per-inbox caps, pause/resume commands, and scheduler leases.
Each inbox warms from a low daily cap (~10-20) up to ~40-50 as clean engagement accrues.
Cross ~2% bounce or ~0.1% complaint on a domain -> auto-pause and quarantine; drain in-flight queued attempts to blocked.
Emergency: a manual kill switch pauses a tenant or the whole fleet; queued work waits, it is not dropped.
Rotate to a fresh warmed domain while a quarantined one cools; never blast from a cold replacement.

recap.before: Volume was capped only by API limits.
recap.after: Sends are paced by warmup and reputation across rotated inboxes, with auto-pause on bad signals.
recap.newRisk: Replies need routing to humans, and quality needs measuring.

traps.trap: Pacing sends to the provider's API rate limit.
traps.why: API quota is unrelated to inbox reputation — you can stay under quota and still spike complaints and burn the domain.
traps.instead: Derive caps from warmup state and live bounce/spam rates; lease queued work only when a warmed inbox has headroom.

traps.trap: No emergency pause or kill switch.
traps.why: When reputation drops fast, you need to stop in-flight and queued sends immediately, not wait for the next campaign edit.
traps.instead: A pause/quarantine command that transitions queued attempts to blocked and holds the durable queue.

steps.title: 5. Qualified Handoff, Audit & Evaluation
description: Close the loop with people and measurement. Inbound replies are classified first — never routed straight to a rep: unsubscribe/angry intent suppresses and stops the sequence, while qualified replies and out-of-scope objections hand off to the CRM's owning rep, with the CRM updated as the system of record. Every enrichment source, draft, suppression check, approval, and send is recorded in an append-only audit.
Evaluate reply quality and qualification accuracy with trajectory eval, and watch deliverability metrics, so a high send volume never hides a reputation slide or weak targeting.

view.caption: Inbound replies hit the orchestrator as a webhook and are classified first; qualified ones hand off to the owning rep, the CRM updates, and every action is appended to an immutable audit. Replies never bypass classification straight to a rep.

steps.decisionPrompt: When should the agent stop and hand a conversation to a human?

concepts.definition: Routing a qualified or out-of-scope conversation from the agent to a human rep.
concepts.whyItMatters: Agents open and qualify; humans close — the handoff point is a design decision.
concepts.example: A prospect asks for pricing detail -> hand to the owning rep with context.

whyNow: Handoff, audit, and eval are what make the outreach engine trustworthy and improvable.

deepDives.title: Why deliverability is the real metric
points: Spam-complaint and bounce thresholds are hard limits; cross them and reputation drops.
Measure reply/qualification quality, not just send volume.
Audit every send for compliance defensibility.

deepDives.title: Reply edge cases and the handoff decision
points: Out-of-office: don't treat as a reply; pause and retry after the return date.
Ambiguous intent: hand to a human rather than guessing a qualification.
Angry / 'remove me': treat as opt-out — suppress globally and stop the sequence.
Account-owner mismatch: route to the CRM's owning rep, not the campaign owner.
The agent opens and qualifies; a human closes — the handoff point is a deliberate design choice.

recap.after: Qualified replies hand off, the CRM updates, and quality is evaluated.
recap.newRisk: Other verticals (HR) gate on fairness rather than reputation — see the rest of the series.

traps.trap: Routing the handoff to the campaign owner instead of the account owner.
traps.why: The rep who launched the campaign often isn't the rep who owns the account in the CRM; the prospect gets a stranger and the real owner is blindsided.
traps.instead: Route to the CRM's owning rep for the account, not whoever started the sequence.

traps.trap: Treating an out-of-office auto-reply as positive intent.
traps.why: An OOO bounce-back is not a human reply; qualifying it creates a false positive and a premature handoff.
traps.instead: Detect OOO, don't classify it as a reply — pause the sequence and retry after the return date.

traps.trap: Missing an unsubscribe expressed inside a normal reply.
traps.why: ‘take me off your list’ in free text is a legal opt-out even without clicking unsubscribe; ignoring it keeps mailing a suppressed person.
traps.instead: Classify reply intent for opt-out/‘remove me’ and suppress globally + stop the sequence before any handoff.

traps.why: Blindly writing stage/owner/notes can clobber a rep's hand-curated data and create sync conflicts.
traps.instead: Write through the CRM conflict-resolution path (external version check); append context, don't overwrite curated fields.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Sales Platform
finalDesign.description: Intent signals and ICP lists enter through the sales API; the SDR orchestrator enriches each prospect through an async provider waterfall (cached, provenance-bearing), identity-resolves and dedupes against the CRM (the system of record), and drafts a personalized sequence in a research agent. A rep approves a specific immutable draft version, which enqueues the send on a durable send queue rather than delivering directly. A deterministic send gate then re-checks the global identity-resolved suppression list and compliance (CAN-SPAM/GDPR; TCPA when SMS/phone is enabled) immediately before delivery, with idempotency keys on the send attempt. The deliverability manager rotates and warms domains/inboxes, derives per-inbox caps from warmup state and live bounce/spam rates, auto-pauses domains that cross thresholds, and leases queued work only when a warmed inbox has headroom. Provider callbacks (delivered/bounce/complaint/unsubscribe) are deduped and immediately update global suppression. Inbound replies arrive as their own webhook and are classified by the orchestrator before any handoff — they never route straight to a rep; qualified ones hand off to the CRM's owning rep, the CRM is updated with conflict resolution, every action is recorded in an append-only audit_events trail (actor, action, object, decision, policy/source provenance — plus consent basis, opt-out source, and footer-template version for compliance defensibility), and reply quality plus deliverability are evaluated. The identity/token broker issues scoped, short-lived act-as-rep tokens so CRM writes and sends carry delegated, attributable authority rather than the rep's own credentials. The distinct queues — approval, send/scheduler, provider-retry, and the webhook/event stream — each carry different safety properties. CRM/identity, guardrails, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations, scoped per tenant; the gate protects sender reputation and compliance.

functional.how: Signals plus a provider waterfall, deduped against the CRM.
Bidirectional writes to the system of record.
Suppression, compliance, and approval before delivery.
Qualified replies route to a human rep.

nonFunctional.how: Rotation, warmup, per-inbox caps, bounce/spam monitoring.
Global identity-resolved suppression at the gate.
CAN-SPAM (address + opt-out, footer-template version) and GDPR (consent/lawful basis, deletion) enforced before send; TCPA when phone/SMS is enabled.
Per-tenant data partitioning and access control; enriched/web text screened as untrusted at the guardrail.

A first-class append-only audit_events trail (actor, action, object, decision, policy_version, source provenance) records enrichment sources, immutable approved drafts, suppression checks, approvals, send attempts, and deduped webhooks, retained for compliance defensibility.

say: Frame the gate as protecting sender reputation and compliance — the reputation tier of the gradient.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the mass-blast and explain how it burns the domain.
CRM plus waterfall enrichment, then the send gate (suppression, compliance, approval).
Domain/inbox rotation, warmup, reputation-driven rate limits distinct from API limits.
Qualified handoff, audit, and reply/deliverability eval.

expectations: Agent enriches and drafts personalized outreach.
Knows sends need approval and opt-out checks.
Send gate with global suppression; deliverability basics.
Treats deliverability as first-class infra with reputation-driven limits.
Designs global identity-resolved suppression and approve-before-send.
Measures reply quality, not just volume; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you identity-resolve a prospect across providers to dedupe and suppress correctly?
How do you warm a new sending domain without tanking reputation?
Where does the agent stop and a human rep take over?
A prospect opts out after a rep approves but before the queued send fires — what happens?
An email provider times out after accepting a message; how do you avoid double-sending on retry?
How do you resolve a CRM sync conflict when a rep edited a field the system also wants to write?
How does the reputation gate compare to the legal vertical's work-product gate?
